summary:
The Meiji Restoration of 1868 was one of the most remarkable political and social transformations in modern history: the rapid, deliberate modernization of Japan from a feudal society governed by a military shogunate into an industrialized, constitutionally governed nation-state capable of competing with the Western imperial powers on their own terms. Within a single generation, Japan was transformed from a country that had executed people for building Western-style ships into one of the world's leading naval and military powers.

The Restoration's immediate context was the crisis triggered by the [[event:2589|Perry Expedition]] of 1853–54, when American Commodore Matthew Perry's 'Black Ships' forced Japan to open its ports and sign unequal treaties. The humiliating demonstration of Japan's military helplessness against Western technology delegitimized the [[event:2588|Tokugawa Shogunate]], which had governed Japan for over 250 years, and emboldened opponents who rallied behind the slogan 'Revere the Emperor, Expel the Barbarians.' The [[event:2590|Boshin War]] of 1868–69 ended with the defeat of the Shogunate's forces and the restoration of political authority to the teenage Emperor Meiji.

The new Meiji government quickly abandoned the 'expel the barbarians' slogan and replaced it with a radically different program: 'Civilization and Enlightenment' (Bunmei Kaika) and 'Rich Country, Strong Army' (Fukoku Kyohei). The oligarchs who dominated the Meiji government — mostly young samurai from the domains of Satsuma and Choshu — were pragmatic modernizers who understood that Japan's survival required the adoption of Western technology, institutions, and organizational methods.

The transformation was sweeping. The feudal domain system was abolished and replaced with a centralized prefectural administration. The [[event:2619|samurai class was abolished]] as a hereditary military caste, and a Western-style conscript army was created in its place — a change that triggered the [[event:2591|Satsuma Rebellion]] of 1877, the last significant samurai uprising. A national education system was built, compulsory for all children. Western legal codes, inspired by French and German models, replaced traditional legal systems. Railways and telegraph lines were constructed. A national bank and paper currency were established.

The political structure was formalized with the [[event:2612|Meiji Constitution]] of 1889, which established a constitutional monarchy modeled partly on the German imperial system, with an elected parliament (Diet) operating alongside a powerful emperor and executive. The constitution preserved imperial sovereignty while creating representative institutions, a balance that suited the oligarchs' desire for controlled modernization rather than popular democracy.

The results were extraordinary. Japan industrialized rapidly, built a modern navy, and in the [[event:2550|First Sino-Japanese War]] of 1894–95 defeated China — demonstrating that an Asian nation had successfully adopted Western military methods. The [[event:2561|Russo-Japanese War]] of 1904–05 brought even more stunning results when Japan became the first non-Western country to defeat a major European power in modern warfare. The Meiji Restoration stands as the most successful example of rapid, state-directed modernization in history, and its lessons have been studied and debated by developing nations ever since.
